These verses are to be joined with the previous chapter. Woe to the powerful leaders who devise and decree unjust laws! And woe to the lower officials who draft them and record them! But what will sinners do? Where will they flee?

See what a change sin made. The king of Assyria, in his pride, thought to act according to his own will. Worldly rulers are tools of God’s plan. God intends to correct his people for their hypocrisy and bring them closer to him; but is that Sennacherib's intention? No; he intends to satisfy his own greed and ambition. The Assyrian boasts about the great things he has done to other nations, through his own policies and power. He does not realize that it is God who makes him what he is and puts authority into his hand. He accomplished all of this easily; no one moved a wing, or cried like birds when their nests are robbed. Because he conquered Samaria, he thinks Jerusalem will fall as well. It was regrettable that Jerusalem set up idols, and we cannot be surprised that the Gentiles surpassed her in doing so. But is it not equally foolish for Christians to imitate the ways of the world in frivolous things instead of focusing on what brings them special honor? For a tool to boast or strive against him who made it would not be more out of place than for Sennacherib to vaunt himself against Jehovah. When God brings his people into trouble, it is to bring sin to their remembrance, humble them, and awaken them to a sense of duty; this must be the result, even the removal of sin. When these things are achieved through suffering, the affliction will be removed in mercy. This attack on Zion and Jerusalem should come to nothing. God will be like a fire to consume those who do evil, both soul and body. The destruction will be as when a standard-bearer weakens, and those who follow are put to shame. Who is able to stand before this great and holy Lord God?

Through our afflictions we may learn not to rely on people. Only those who return to God in truth, not just in outward appearance, can stay with comfort. God will justly bring this devastation upon a provoking people, but he will graciously set limits to it. It is against the mind and will of God that his people, no matter what happens, should give way to fear. God’s anger against his people lasts only for a moment; when it turns away from us, we need not fear the fury of humans. The rod with which he corrected his people will not only be put aside but thrown into the fire. To encourage God's people, the prophet reminds them of what God had previously done against the enemies of his church. God’s people will be delivered from the Assyrians. Some believe this refers to the deliverance of the Jews from their captivity; and even further, to the redemption of believers from the tyranny of sin and Satan. And this is “because of the anointing,” for the sake of his people Israel, those believers among them who have received the unction of Divine grace. And for the sake of the Messiah, God’s Anointed One. Here is a prophetic description of Sennacherib's march toward Jerusalem when he threatened to destroy that city. Then the Lord, in whom Hezekiah trusted, cut down his army like cutting through a forest. Let us apply what is written here to similar situations in other ages of Christ’s church. Because of the anointing of our great Redeemer, the yoke of every antichrist must be broken from off his church: and if our souls partake of the unction of the Holy Spirit, complete and eternal deliverances will be secured to us.
